metadata.summary: Advanced Helm templating techniques including library charts, hooks, tests, umbrella charts, and production-ready patterns.

content.title: Helm Advanced Patterns
content.summary: Advanced Helm techniques for production-grade chart development: library charts for reusable templates, hooks for lifecycle intervention (pre-install, post-upgrade, test), Helm test framework for deployment validation, umbrella charts for multi-service composition, and production patterns (checksum annotations, required values, resource protection). Master these patterns to build maintainable, testable charts at enterprise scale.

why.p: Basic Helm covers single-chart deployments. Production systems require advanced patterns: library charts eliminate duplication when multiple application charts share template helpers (e.g., all apps use the same labeling convention); hooks automate pre/post deployment tasks (database migrations, smoke tests, cleanup); tests validate that deployed releases actually work (not just that manifests render). Umbrella charts compose related services (app + database + cache) into a single logical release, enabling coordinated rollouts and version pinning across tiers.

Checksum annotations force pod restarts when ConfigMap or Secret content changes (without manual delete pod). Required values with clear error messages prevent silent failures. Resource protection policies (helm.sh/resource-policy: keep) prevent accidental data loss when uninstalling releases (e.g., PersistentVolumeClaim should survive chart deletion). These patterns scale Helm from simple deploys to enterprise-grade deployments supporting thousands of microservices.

list.item: Creating library charts to share template logic (naming, labels, common patterns) across 5+ application charts to reduce duplication.
Implementing lifecycle hooks for database migrations (pre-upgrade), smoke tests (post-install), or cleanup (pre-delete).
Validating deployed releases with Helm tests that confirm connectivity, health checks, and basic functionality.
Composing multi-service applications (app + PostgreSQL + Redis) with coordinated deployments using umbrella charts.
Enforcing production practices: config checksums, required values validation, resource protection, Helm linting in CI/CD.
Simple single-service deployments — basic Helm covers this; library charts add overhead without benefit.
Lifecycle operations requiring external systems (Kubernetes operators, custom controllers) — hooks are limited to Kubernetes Jobs/Pods.
Complex multi-service orchestration with inter-service coordination — use Kubernetes operators or GitOps controllers (ArgoCD, Flux) for advanced scenarios.
Services with independent release cycles that need to be deployed separately — avoid umbrella charts; manage at GitOps level instead.

section.name: library-charts
section.p: Library charts are reusable templates not deployed directly but included by other charts. Set type: library in Chart.yaml. Dependent charts access parent context and library templates. Eliminates duplication of naming conventions, labels, and helper functions across application charts.

rule.statement: Library charts MUST have type: library in Chart.yaml and MUST NOT contain values.yaml or templates/*.yaml resources (no deployments, services, etc.; only _helpers.tpl). Library chart names MUST use common- or lib- prefix to signal non-deployable status. Dependent charts MUST declare the library in Chart.yaml dependencies section. Library templates MUST use named templates ({{ define "libname.helper" }}) to avoid accidental output. Library charts MUST NOT be installed directly (helm install will fail if type: library is set). Documentation MUST clearly explain which templates are exported and how to use them in dependent charts.
rule.rationale: type: library prevents accidental direct installation (Helm rejects it) and signals to other developers that this chart is a dependency. Named templates prevent output; only explicit {{ include }} calls render content. Prefix (common-, lib-) signals intent in dependency lists. Library charts reduce duplication: 10 app charts each with identical naming/labeling logic = 1 library + 10 apps using it. Maintenance: one place to fix naming bugs or add new labels.

example.title: Library chart with shared helpers
example.note: Library is listed in dependencies. Include is used in app chart templates. Library has zero deployable resources.

example.title: Broken library with deployable resources
example.code:
# library-chart/Chart.yaml type: library # but contains templates/deployment.yaml!
# library-chart/templates/deployment.yaml (wrong) kind: Deployment metadata: name: shared-app # library should not deploy anything
# Result: helm install library-chart fails because type: library
example.note: Library charts must NOT contain deployable resources. Use only named templates in _helpers.tpl.

antipattern.description: Creating a library chart so generic it tries to support all possible Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, StatefulSet, Job, CronJob, etc.) with deeply nested conditionals.
antipattern.reason: A library that generates any resource type with any configuration is no longer a library; it's a meta-templating engine. Keep libraries focused: "shared naming conventions" or "pod security policies" or "common labels". If you find yourself writing 100+ lines of conditionals in _helpers.tpl, split into focused libraries or question whether Helm is the right tool.

section.name: lifecycle-hooks
section.p: Hooks execute Kubernetes resources at specific release lifecycle points: pre-install, post-install, pre-upgrade, post-upgrade, pre-delete, post-delete, pre-rollback, post-rollback, test. Annotate resources with helm.sh/hook to assign them to lifecycle stages. Hook resources (usually Jobs, Pods) are cleaned up according to deletion policies.

rule.statement: Hook resources MUST be annotated with helm.sh/hook, helm.sh/hook-weight (integer for execution order), and helm.sh/hook-delete-policy (before-hook-creation or hook-succeeded). Database migration hooks MUST run pre-upgrade to ensure schema changes are applied before app restart (prevents 5xx errors from mismatched schema). Hooks MUST be idempotent: running the same hook twice must produce the same result (use init containers to check if migration already applied, rather than failing on duplicate). Test hooks (helm.sh/hook: test) MUST validate deployed release (connectivity, health, basic functionality) with exit code 0 = success, non-zero = failure. Hooks MUST inherit Release.Name and Release.Namespace from parent chart to avoid naming conflicts.
rule.rationale: Hooks are useful for automation: migrations pre-upgrade, health checks post-install, cleanup pre-delete. Hook weight (negative to positive) orders execution: weight -5 before weight 0. Idempotency prevents failures on re-runs (Helm retries if hook fails). Test hooks provide continuous validation: after helm install, helm test validates the deployment works. Naming with Release.Name ensures hooks don't conflict when multiple releases coexist.

example.title: Pre-upgrade migration hook
example.code: # templates/migrate.yaml apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: Job metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-migrate-{{ .Release.Revision }} annotations: "helm.sh/hook": pre-upgrade "helm.sh/hook-weight": "-5" "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": hook-succeeded spec: template: spec: containers: - name: migrate image: myapp:{{ .Values.image.tag }} command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"] env: - name: DATABASE_URL valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: app-secrets key: database-url restartPolicy: Never backoffLimit: 1
example.note: Runs pre-upgrade, deleted after success. Release.Revision ensures unique Job names on retries.

example.title: Post-install smoke test hook
example.code: # templates/tests/test-smoke.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-test-smoke annotations: "helm.sh/hook": test "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": hook-succeeded spec: containers: - name: test image: curlimages/curl:latest command: - /bin/sh - -c - | curl -f http://{{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}:{{ .Values.service.port }}/health echo "Health check passed" restartPolicy: Never
example.note: Runs helm test. Curl health endpoint; exit 0 = test passes, exit non-0 = test fails.

example.title: Non-idempotent migration
example.code: # hook command in migration job command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"] # If called twice, fails: "column already exists" # Correct: use framework migration tools that check if migration already applied
example.note: Migrations must be idempotent. Use Django/Rails/Alembic which track applied migrations.

antipattern.description: Using hooks for operations that should be handled by Kubernetes controllers or sidecar patterns.
antipattern.reason: Hooks are good for one-time setup (migrations, smoke tests). For ongoing operations (metrics collection, log shipping, secret rotation), use init containers, sidecar containers, or Kubernetes operators. Hooks run at specific lifecycle points; controllers run continuously.

section.name: helm-tests
section.p: Helm tests validate deployed releases by running test pods. helm test executes pods annotated with helm.sh/hook: test. Test passes if pod exits with code 0. Tests confirm deployed resources are healthy and functional, not just that manifests render.

rule.statement: Test pods MUST be stored in templates/tests/ directory with descriptive names (test-connection.yaml, test-smoke.yaml). Each test pod MUST have restartPolicy: Never and exit code 0 on success. Tests MUST validate core functionality (connectivity, health checks, basic operations) not implementation details. Test pod names MUST include {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} to avoid conflicts. Tests MUST be run in CI/CD pipeline after every deployment (helm install → helm test → if fails, rollback). Documentation MUST list test coverage and what each test validates.
rule.rationale: Tests run after install/upgrade, confirming the deployment is healthy before marking it complete. Exit code 0 = test passes; non-zero = test fails. Test pods are temporary (helm.sh/hook-delete-policy: hook-succeeded cleans them up after completion). Tests in CI prevent broken deployments from reaching production. Core functionality tests are more valuable than implementation details (test "GET /health returns 200" not "ConfigMap mounted at /etc/config").

example.title: Comprehensive test suite
example.code:
# templates/tests/test-connection.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-test-connection annotations: "helm.sh/hook": test spec: containers: - name: wget image: busybox command: ['wget'] args: ['{{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}:{{ .Values.service.port }}'] restartPolicy: Never
# templates/tests/test-database.yaml apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: Job metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-test-db annotations: "helm.sh/hook": test spec: template: spec: containers: - name: test image: myapp:{{ .Values.image.tag }} command: ["python", "-c"] args: - | import psycopg2 conn = psycopg2.connect(os.environ['DATABASE_URL']) cur = conn.cursor() cur.execute('SELECT 1') assert cur.fetchone()[0] == 1 print('Database connectivity OK') restartPolicy: Never
example.note: Run: helm install myapp . && helm test myapp. Both tests must exit 0.

example.title: Fragile tests
example.code:
# Test checks implementation, not functionality apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: test-config annotations: "helm.sh/hook": test spec: containers: - name: test command: ["grep", "CONFIG_VALUE=production", "/etc/config/app.conf"] # Fails if ConfigMap format changes, even if app still works
# Test has no timeout; hangs forever if service unreachable apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: test-hang spec: containers: - name: test command: ["curl", "http://unreachable-service"] # curl waits forever; test never completes
example.note: Test functionality (service responds), not configuration. Add timeout: 30 to prevent hangs.

antipattern.description: Tests that succeed without actually validating anything (e.g., echo "OK" as test command).
antipattern.reason: Tests should validate actual deployment health. A test that always passes is worse than no test; it gives false confidence. Test at least: connectivity (service is reachable), health checks (app reports healthy), or basic functionality (GET /api/health, database query, etc.).

section.name: umbrella-charts
section.p: Umbrella charts compose multiple application charts into a single deployment unit. Useful for coordinating related services (app + PostgreSQL + Redis) with version pinning and shared configuration. Declare dependencies in Chart.yaml; configure with values.yaml. Each dependency can be independently enabled/disabled.

rule.statement: Umbrella charts MUST declare all dependencies in Chart.yaml with exact versions and repositories. Each dependency MUST be gated by a condition (condition: postgresql.enabled) to support optional components. Values MUST be scoped by dependency name (postgresql.*, backend.*, frontend.*) to avoid collisions. Global values (imageRegistry, imagePullSecrets) MUST be defined in .global namespace and referenced by dependent charts. Umbrella charts MUST be used only for related components with shared deployment lifecycle; independent services MUST NOT be bundled. Documentation MUST list all dependencies, their versions, and configuration options.
rule.rationale: Umbrella charts simplify deployments: helm install myapp umbrella-chart deploys app + database + cache in one shot. Conditions enable staging (PostgreSQL disabled; use managed Cloud SQL) vs development (PostgreSQL included). Global values prevent duplication: registry defined once, inherited by all subcharts. However, umbrella charts create tight coupling; don't use if services need independent release cycles or are managed by different teams.

example.title: Umbrella chart structure
example.code:
# Chart.yaml apiVersion: v2 name: myapp-umbrella version: 1.0.0 dependencies: - name: myapp version: "1.0.0" repository: "file://charts/myapp" - name: postgresql version: "12.1.2" repository: "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami" condition: postgresql.enabled - name: redis version: "17.0.0" repository: "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami" condition: redis.enabled
# values.yaml global: imageRegistry: myregistry.io imagePullSecrets: - name: regcred
myapp: replicaCount: 3 image: repository: myapp
postgresql: enabled: true # dev: enabled; prod: disabled auth: postgresPassword: dev-password
redis: enabled: true auth: enabled: false
example.note: Global values inherited by subcharts. Each dependency can be toggled.

example.title: Umbrella bundling unrelated services
example.code: # Umbrella mixing app, payments platform, and third-party metrics dependencies: - name: myapp - name: stripe-connector - name: datadog-agent # If Stripe API breaks, myapp umbrella becomes undeployable # If metrics team updates Datadog, myapp versions are tied to it # Use separate releases for independent services
example.note: Umbrella = related components. Independent services = separate releases.

antipattern.description: Creating an umbrella chart that requires complex inter-chart communication or ordering (e.g., backend must wait for database to initialize).
antipattern.reason: Helm is not an orchestrator. It deploys all charts in parallel. If ordering is required (app waits for database), use init containers (initContainer that waits for database to be ready) or readiness probes (app reports not ready if database unavailable), not Helm hooks. For truly complex orchestration, use Kubernetes operators.

section.name: production-patterns
section.p: Production patterns ensure reliability and prevent common issues: checksum annotations force pod restarts on config changes, required values catch missing configuration early, resource protection prevents data loss on uninstall, and linting in CI catches syntax errors before deployment.

rule.statement: Templates MUST include checksum/config annotations on Deployment/StatefulSet pod templates to force restarts when ConfigMap or Secret changes (prevents stale config). Required values MUST use {{ required "message" .Values.critical_value }} to provide clear error on missing configuration. Resources (PVC, databases, persistent data) MUST be annotated with helm.sh/resource-policy: keep to prevent deletion on release uninstall. All templates MUST pass helm lint in CI/CD before merge; linting MUST enforce naming conventions, required fields, and no deprecated APIs. Charts MUST use values.schema.json to define configuration structure, types, and validation rules for IDE support and early error detection.
rule.rationale: Checksum annotations (sha256sum of ConfigMap/Secret) trigger pod recreation when config changes; without them, pods continue using old mounted config until manually restarted. Required values catch typos early ("mistmatch" in -f values.yaml file name = clear error, not silent no-op). Resource protection prevents accidental data loss: uninstalling app chart should NOT delete its database. Helm lint in CI prevents merging broken charts. values.schema.json enables VS Code/IDE validation before deployment.

example.title: Checksum and resource protection
example.note: Checksum annotation triggers pod restart on ConfigMap change. Helm.sh/resource-policy: keep prevents PVC deletion on uninstall.

example.title: Required values and schema
example.note: Required values fail with clear error. values.schema.json enables VS Code validation.

example.title: Missing checksums and protection
example.code:
# No checksum annotation spec: template: spec: containers: - name: app # Config changes don't trigger restart; pods use stale config
# No resource protection apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: app-data # helm uninstall deletes PVC and all data inside
example.note: Add checksum/config annotations. Add helm.sh/resource-policy: keep for persistent resources.

antipattern.description: Skipping helm lint in CI or linting with --strict disabled because some warnings fail the build.
antipattern.reason: Helm lint catches real issues: deprecated API versions, missing required fields, misnamed resources. Warnings should be addressed (e.g., missing NOTES.txt, no app version in labels). Disabling --strict is a code smell; fix the warnings instead. Lint should be a required CI gate.

template.purpose: Production-ready template snippets for library charts, hooks, tests, umbrella composition, and advanced patterns

section.name: advanced-templating-techniques
section.p: Go templating in Helm supports functions, conditionals, loops, and pipelines. Mastering templating enables building flexible, maintainable charts. Common patterns: checksum annotations (force pod restart on config change), required values (validation), template evaluation (dynamic templates), resource protection.

rule.statement: Templates MUST use named helpers ({{ include "..." }}) for DRY compliance; avoid repeating YAML snippets. Complex conditionals (3+ levels) MUST be refactored into separate partials or require clear documentation. Template functions (default, required, toYaml, nindent) MUST escape YAML correctly (toYaml | nindent for nested objects). Checksum annotations MUST be included on Deployment/StatefulSet pod templates to force restarts when ConfigMap/Secret changes (without manual pod deletion). Required values MUST be validated with {{ required "message" .Values.field }} for clear error messages. Comments in templates MUST explain non-obvious logic to aid future maintainers.
rule.rationale: Named helpers reduce duplication and enable reusability. Complex conditionals reduce readability; split into smaller partials. YAML escaping (toYaml | nindent) prevents malformed manifests. Checksums on pod templates force restarts automatically when config changes (without checksum, pods use cached mounted ConfigMap). required() fails fast with clear error, not silent failures. Comments document intent: why this conditional exists, what values it expects.

example.title: Checksum annotation for config changes
example.code:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-config data: app.conf: | {{ .Values.appConfig | nindent 4 }}
--- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} spec: template: metadata: annotations: # Force pod restart when ConfigMap changes checksum/config: {{ include (print $.Template.BasePath "/configmap.yaml") . | sha256sum }} # Or check multiple config files checksum/all: {{ include (print $.Template.BasePath "/configmap.yaml") . | sha256sum }}{{ include (print $.Template.BasePath "/secret.yaml") . | sha256sum }} spec: containers: - name: app volumeMounts: - name: config mountPath: /etc/config volumes: - name: config configMap: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-config
example.note: Checksum triggers pod restart when ConfigMap content changes. Without it, pods use cached mounted config until manually deleted.

example.title: No checksum; stale config
example.code:
# No checksum annotation spec: template: metadata: # No annotations with checksum spec: containers: - name: app volumeMounts: - name: config mountPath: /etc/config volumes: - name: config configMap: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-config
# Update ConfigMap value: # kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml # updates ConfigMap # helm upgrade myapp . # updates ConfigMap but NOT pod mounting it # Result: pod continues using old config value until manually deleted
example.note: Always add checksum/config annotation to force pod restart on config change.

antipattern.description: Using template evaluation ({{ tpl .Values.template . }}) to dynamically load template strings from values.
antipattern.reason: Templating values makes debugging difficult: errors occur at runtime, not at chart development time. Values become code, breaking separation of data and logic. Avoid {{ tpl }}; keep values simple (strings, numbers, objects), not templates. If you need dynamic logic, implement it in Kubernetes controllers or use Kustomize for post-Helm transformation.

section.name: hook-best-practices
section.p: Hooks automate lifecycle operations but are limited to Kubernetes Jobs and Pods. Best practices: idempotent operations, proper cleanup policies, hook weight ordering, and comprehensive error handling for visibility.

rule.statement: Hooks MUST be idempotent: running the same hook twice produces same result (migrations check if already applied, cleanup removes stale resources). Hook jobs MUST have backoffLimit: 0 or 1 to fail fast on error (no retries). Hook deletion policy MUST be specified: hook-succeeded (cleanup after success), before-hook-creation (delete old hook before creating new one). Hooks MUST log output for debugging; use kubectl logs on hook pod to diagnose failures. Pre-upgrade hooks MUST run before pod restart (weight: -5) to apply schema migrations before app start. Post-install/test hooks MUST validate deployed release works (connectivity, health checks). Hooks MUST NOT rely on external systems (APIs, databases) being fully ready; add retry logic in hook script (not Helm retry).
rule.rationale: Idempotency prevents cascade failures on retries: if migration hook runs twice, second run detects "already applied" and succeeds. backoffLimit: 0 prevents Kubernetes from retrying failed hooks automatically (retry once = two failures, confusing operator). Hook deletion policy prevents accumulating stale hook pods. Logging enables debugging. Pre-upgrade hooks run before app deployment, preventing 5xx errors from schema mismatch. Post-install tests confirm deployment succeeded. External dependency handling in script (poll loop with timeout) is safer than relying on Kubernetes readiness probes being ready before hook runs.

example.title: Idempotent pre-upgrade migration
example.code: apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: Job metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-migrate-{{ .Release.Revision }} annotations: "helm.sh/hook": pre-upgrade "helm.sh/hook-weight": "-5" "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": hook-succeeded spec: template: spec: containers: - name: migrate image: myapp:{{ .Values.image.tag }} command: - /bin/sh - -c - | # Idempotent migration: check if already applied python manage.py showmigrations | grep -q "0001_initial \[X\]" if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then echo "Migration already applied" exit 0 fi # Apply migration python manage.py migrate if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then echo "Migration failed" exit 1 fi env: - name: DATABASE_URL valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: app-secrets key: database-url restartPolicy: Never backoffLimit: 0 # Fail fast on error
example.note: Idempotent: check if migration already applied before running. Fail fast with backoffLimit: 0.

section.name: library-chart-patterns
section.p: Library charts are non-deployable template repositories shared across application charts. Eliminates duplication of naming conventions, labels, and common patterns. Library charts are dependencies, not directly installed.

rule.statement: Library charts MUST have type: library in Chart.yaml to prevent direct installation. Library charts MUST NOT contain values.yaml or deployable templates (no deployment.yaml, service.yaml). Library charts MUST contain only _helpers.tpl with named templates ({{ define "libname.something" }}). Dependent charts MUST declare library in dependencies section with file://../library-chart path (for co-located development) or HTTP repository URL (for published libraries). Library templates MUST use clear naming: libname.fullname, libname.labels, libname.selectorLabels to signal intent. Documentation MUST explain which templates are exported and how dependent charts use them via {{ include }}.
rule.rationale: type: library prevents accidental installation (Helm rejects it). Named templates-only prevents output; library doesn't deploy anything, just provides reusable template functions. Co-location (file://) enables rapid development; HTTP repo enables external teams to depend on published library. Clear naming signals library intent: libname.* is from library, not local templates. Documentation enables dependent charts to discover and use exported functions.

example.title: Library chart structure
example.note: Library has zero deployable resources. Dependent imports and uses via {{ include }}.

antipattern.description: Creating a single library chart so generic it supports all Kubernetes resources with deep conditional logic.
antipattern.reason: A library serving all use cases becomes unmaintainable. Keep libraries focused: "naming conventions", "common labels", "pod security policies". If you find yourself writing 100+ lines of conditionals, split into focused libraries or question if Helm templating is the right tool for such complexity. Kustomize or Kubernetes controllers may be better for advanced composition.

section.name: security-patterns-in-charts
section.p: Security in Helm charts: Pod Security Policies/Standards, Network Policies, RBAC, secret management, container security (read-only filesystem, drop capabilities, non-root user).

rule.statement: Pod specs MUST run as non-root user (securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000). Containers MUST have read-only root filesystem (readOnlyRootFilesystem: true) with writable tmpfs for temp files. Capabilities MUST be dropped except those explicitly needed (securityContext.capabilities.drop: ALL, add: [NET_BIND_SERVICE]). Secrets MUST NOT be stored in values.yaml; use external secret manager (Vault, Sealed Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager) with operator injecting secrets at deploy time. Network Policies MUST restrict pod-to-pod communication (allow only necessary ingress/egress). RBAC MUST grant minimum necessary permissions (avoid wildcard *). Pod Security Standards (PSS) SHOULD be enforced at namespace level (baseline, restricted). Documentation MUST explain security posture and compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC2) supported by chart.
rule.rationale: Non-root user prevents container escape escalating to host root. Read-only filesystem prevents malware persistence. Dropped capabilities reduce attack surface. External secret managers prevent secrets in Git. Network Policies segment traffic. RBAC prevents lateral movement. PSS labels (restricted = stricter, baseline = permissive) enforce pod security org-wide. Documentation enables operators to assess compliance.

example.title: Secure pod specification
example.code: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} spec: template: spec: serviceAccountName: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} containers: - name: app image: {{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }} securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true runAsUser: 1000 runAsGroup: 3000 readOnlyRootFilesystem: true allowPrivilegeEscalation: false capabilities: drop: - ALL seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault volumeMounts: - name: tmp mountPath: /tmp - name: config mountPath: /etc/config readOnly: true volumes: - name: tmp emptyDir: {} - name: config configMap: name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-config # Pod Security Standards label securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
example.note: Non-root user, read-only filesystem, dropped capabilities, no privilege escalation. seccompProfile enables seccomp profile (restrict syscalls).

antipattern.description: Running container as root (UID 0) to simplify permission issues.
antipattern.reason: Root container = container escape = host root access. Fix permission issues by running as dedicated user, not root. Use initContainer (runs as root once) to change file ownership, then run main app as non-root.

section.name: chart-validation-ci-cd
section.p: Charts must pass validation in CI/CD before merge. Validation includes syntax (helm lint), manifest output (helm template), API validation (helm install --dry-run=server), and functional tests (helm test). Automated checks catch errors early, preventing broken deployments.

rule.statement: All charts MUST pass helm lint with no warnings (strict mode). Chart templates MUST be validated with helm template to verify manifest output. Deployed manifests MUST be validated server-side (helm install --dry-run=server) against actual cluster API version. Helm tests MUST confirm deployed release is functional (connectivity, health, basic operations). Test results MUST be captured in CI logs for debugging. Integration tests SHOULD verify inter-chart communication in umbrella deployments (app can reach database, cache). All validation MUST run in CI/CD; only passing charts are mergeable. Documentation MUST list validation steps and failure troubleshooting.
rule.rationale: helm lint catches YAML errors, deprecated APIs, missing fields early. helm template verifies templating without contacting cluster (safe, fast). --dry-run=server validates against actual API version (catches API compatibility issues). Helm tests confirm app health post-deploy (pod may start but service unreachable). Integration tests in umbrella charts verify subcharts communicate correctly. CI gates prevent broken charts reaching production. Documentation enables operators to understand and reproduce validation locally.

example.title: Comprehensive CI/CD validation
example.note: Sequential validation: lint → template → API → deploy → test → cleanup. Failure at any stage blocks merge.

example.title: Insufficient CI validation
example.code:
#!/bin/bash # Minimal (inadequate) validation helm lint ./mychart # Only syntax; no API or functional validation
# Chart passes "validation" but fails in production: # - API compatibility issue (helm lint doesn't catch) # - Pod crashes on startup (no functional test) # - Database unreachable (no integration test)
example.note: Comprehensive validation catches real issues. Lint-only misses API, deployment, and functional problems.

antipattern.description: Skipping --dry-run=server validation because "lint should catch everything".
antipattern.reason: helm lint catches syntax and structure errors. It does NOT validate against actual Kubernetes API version or custom resources (CRDs). A chart valid in 1.20 may fail in 1.28 if API changed. Always run --dry-run=server against target cluster version.

section.name: troubleshooting-advanced-scenarios
section.p: Debugging complex Helm scenarios: dependency conflicts, hook failures, resource orphaning, rollback incompatibility, and performance issues. Tools: helm history, helm get values, kubectl describe, logs, events.

rule.statement: Release history MUST be preserved (do not delete releases) for audit and rollback capability. Release status MUST be checked with helm status and kubectl describe pod before diagnosing failures. Hook failures MUST be inspected via kubectl logs on hook pod. Failed pods MUST be described to identify missing resources, permission errors, or image issues. Cluster events (kubectl get events -n NAMESPACE) provide often-missed context: ImagePullBackOff, InsufficientMemory, etc. Rollback MUST be tested in staging before applying to production. Performance issues MUST be measured (kubectl top pod, Prometheus metrics) not assumed.
rule.rationale: Release history enables instant rollback to known good state. helm status shows current release state and recent changes. kubectl describe reveals resource issues (missing ConfigMap, RBAC error, image not found). Logs show application errors. Cluster events capture system-level issues (memory pressure, storage errors) that pod logs don't show. Performance debugging without measurement is guesswork; measure, hypothesize, fix, measure again.

example.title: Debugging failed Helm deployment
example.code:
# 1. Check release history helm history myapp --max=10
# 2. Check current release state helm status myapp
# 3. Check pods kubectl get pods -n myapp kubectl describe pod myapp-xyz -n myapp
# 4. Check events (system-level errors) kubectl get events -n myapp --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
# 5. Check logs kubectl logs deployment/myapp -n myapp --tail=100 kubectl logs deployment/myapp -n myapp --previous # crashed pod logs
# 6. Check resource availability kubectl top nodes kubectl top pods -n myapp
# 7. If hook failed, check hook pod logs kubectl logs job/myapp-migrate -n myapp
# 8. Rollback if needed helm rollback myapp 1 -n myapp
# 9. Verify rollback worked helm status myapp kubectl get pods -n myapp
example.note: Systematic debugging: history → status → describe → events → logs → metrics → action. Don't guess; measure.

antipattern.description: Rollback without understanding why the upgrade failed, then rolling back again immediately.
antipattern.reason: Rollback is emergency recovery, not diagnosis. Always understand root cause before rolling back. If you roll back without fixing root cause, the same failure will occur on next upgrade. Diagnose (why did it fail?), fix root cause, then upgrade again.

section.name: umbrella-chart-composition
section.p: Umbrella charts coordinate deployment of related services (app + database + cache) with version pinning and shared configuration. Value scoping prevents naming collisions between subcharts.

rule.statement: Umbrella charts MUST declare all dependencies in Chart.yaml with exact versions. Global values MUST be defined for shared config (imageRegistry, imagePullSecrets) accessible by subcharts via .global namespace. Subchart values MUST be scoped by dependency name (postgresql.*, redis.*) to prevent collisions. Each dependency MUST be optional via condition (condition: postgresql.enabled) to support different deployment topologies (dev: embedded PostgreSQL, prod: managed Cloud SQL). Umbrella chart MUST NOT contain templates/ directly (no deployments, services); dependencies provide all deployable resources. Documentation MUST list all dependencies, their versions, and configuration options with examples.
rule.rationale: Exact versions ensure reproducible installs; flexible ranges introduce hidden breaking changes. Global values prevent duplication: registry defined once, inherited by all subcharts via .global. Scoped values prevent naming collisions: postgresql.replicas and redis.replicas coexist without conflict. Conditions enable topology flexibility: same chart works in dev (embedded DB) and prod (managed DB). Subcharts providing all templates prevents umbrella from managing both infrastructure and subcharts (separation of concerns). Documentation enables operators to understand and customize multi-service deployments.

example.title: Umbrella chart with optional components
example.code:
# Chart.yaml apiVersion: v2 name: myapp-umbrella version: 1.0.0 dependencies: - name: myapp version: "1.0.0" repository: "file://charts/myapp" - name: postgresql version: "12.1.2" repository: "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami" condition: postgresql.enabled - name: redis version: "17.0.0" repository: "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami" condition: redis.enabled
# values.yaml global: imageRegistry: myregistry.io imagePullSecrets: - name: regcred
myapp: replicaCount: 1 image: repository: myapp
postgresql: enabled: true # dev: true; prod: false (use managed) auth: postgresPassword: dev-password primary: resources: requests: memory: 256Mi
redis: enabled: true replica: replicaCount: 1
# values-prod.yaml (override for production) myapp: replicaCount: 5 resources: requests: memory: 512Mi
postgresql: enabled: false # Use managed Cloud SQL instead
redis: enabled: false # Use managed Memorystore instead
example.note: Same chart works for dev (embedded services) and prod (managed services) via conditions.
